sub_topics.title: Introduction to Geography

instructional_blocks.hook: Imagine a world where the physical environment we know today no longer exist, how would geography shape the relationships, cultures, and economies of the people living in those regions?
teacher_steps: Take learners on field trip to the local environment highlighting unique geographical features, cultures and the environments
Cluster the learners in small groups to discuss the concepts of Geography
Display World map or globe/pictures or videos of geographical features
Let learners identify geographical features
Each group to share key ideas to the class
Key responses to be written down by grouping similar ideas
Learner responses should be connected to cement the concept Geography
learner_steps: Participating in small groups discussions about Geography concepts
Interacting with globe and maps to identify physical and culture features
Identifying geographical features from displayed materials
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Geography is the study of the Earth's surface, environment and how humans interact with their surroundings. Geography influences human life; where people live, what they eat and how they build communities. Geography uses tools such as Maps, GIS, GPS, and Remote sensing systems as essential tools to help people navigate and understand the world. Every place on Earth is unique shaped by its physical and human characteristics. Geography helps solve real-world problems such as climate change, deforestation and disaster management.

teacher_steps: Arrange desks in pairs or small groups for learners to collaborate
Display World map or globe showing features of physical and Human Geography
Allow learners to observe the local environment and categorise physical and human features (such as mountains, rivers, farms, towns and waterfalls)
learner_steps: Identifying and classifying physical and cultural features
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Geography is divided into two main branches: Physical and Human Geography. Physical Geography studies natural features and processes. Some sub-branches of physical Geography include climatology, hydrology, geomorphology. Human Geography studies human activities and their impacts on the environment. Some sub-branches of Human geography include cultural geography, political geography, urban geography, population geography. Physical and human Geography are interconnected in that natural features affect human life and human activities impact the environment.

teacher_steps: Arrange desks in clusters, circles or u-shaped for learners to discuss and collaborate on the importance of studying Geography
Use school surroundings such as school gardens, nearby natural parks for field observations on the importance of conservation of the environment
learner_steps: Discussing the importance of studying Geography
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Geography helps learners understand current events such as climate change, migration or natural disasters. It enables learners to use maps, globes or images to illustrate geographical concepts. It fosters cultural awareness and global citizenships such as exploring diverse cultures and societies. It develops critical thinking and problem-solving skills. It enhances environmental awareness and sustainability. It connects geographical concepts to learners' daily lives such as weather, food sources and local environments.

sub_topics.short_notes: Geography is an important subject for secondary school learners, providing them with essential knowledge and skills that are applicable in real-life situations. It fosters critical thinking, global awareness and environmental stewardship, preparing learners to navigate and contribute positively to an interconnected World. By understanding geographical principles, learners are better equipped to face the challenges of the future, making Geography not just an academic subject, but a vital component of informed citizenship and personal development.

vocabulary.definition: The study of the Earth's surface, environment and how humans interact with their surroundings
The surroundings or conditions in which a person, animal, or plant lives or operates
The branch of geography dealing with natural features and processes
The branch of geography dealing with how human activity affects or is influenced by the earth's surface

questions: Observe learners' participation in small groups as they interact with the globe and maps to identify physical and culture features
Observe learners' manipulation of tools such as Maps, GPS and Google maps to identify physical and cultural features
Quick quiz question - One thing I learnt about Geography today is……

sub_topics.title: The Earth in the Solar System
competences: Demonstrate understanding of the position of the Earth in the solar system

instructional_blocks.hook: Imagine discovering a planet within our own solar system that, despite having extreme conditions, harbors a form of life completely unlike that of the Earth. How would such a discovery reshape our understanding of life's potential adaptability and boundaries of life sustaining environments?
teacher_steps: Classroom set up in small groups for the learners to discuss and collaborate as they identify elements of the solar system
Learners will work in small groups to observe a poster, chart or diagram of the solar system and discuss the position of the Earth and share their thoughts with the whole class

Learners will work in small groups to examine the 2 dimension or 3-dimension diagrams of the solar system to identify the planets in their order from the sun and determine Earths position (3rd from the Sun)
Learners will work in small groups and use planet cut-outs and glue or cardboard and paint or balls or clay to create a model of the solar system
Each group should explain their model focusing on the Earth's position and its importance
learner_steps: Identifying various elements found in the Solar system (Sun, Planets, meteors)
Observing solar system posters and diagrams
instructional_blocks.short_notes: The Solar system consists of the sun, planets and their moons and smaller objects like asteroids and comets. The Sun is at the centre of the solar system and the Earth is the third planet from it. The Earth is the only planet known to support life due to its right balance of sunlight, temperature, water and atmosphere. All planets including the Earth revolve around the Sun in elliptical orbits. The force of gravity keeps the planets, including the Earth in their orbits around the sun.

teacher_steps: Set up an astronomy corner in the classroom with charts, books, models, pictures and arrange them for group discussion
Discuss what they already know about celestial bodies (sun, moon, stars, planets) and share personal experiences such as seeing a shooting star or an eclipse
Discuss concepts such as phases of the moon, constellations and planetary movements
Create observation areas or points in school fields or playground away from artificial lights
Observe night sky or day sky in order to identify the phases of the moon, planets, meteor showers and constellations
Use Binoculars and telescopes if available and ensure safety of learners
learner_steps: Engaging in observing celestial activities as a hobby or for educational purposes
Recording date, time and weather conditions
Identifying interesting patterns or changes such as brightness of planets
Providing weekly observations of celestial activities and how they differ from each other
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Celestial objects are natural entities that exist in the universe such as stars, planets, moons, asteroids, comets, galaxies. These objects are also referred to as heavenly, astronomical, space, cosmic or stellar objects. The celestial bodies appear to move from east to west due to the Earth's rotation. There are eight planets: Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus and Neptune. Venus, Mars and Jupiter are the only planets visible to the naked eyes. The brightness of the star in the night sky often depends on its distance from Earth and its size. The moon goes through a repeating cycle of phases every month. The solar eclipse happens when the moon passes between the Earth and the Sun, blocking the Sun's light from reaching the Earth.

sub_topics.short_notes: The Earth is the third planet from the Sun in the Solar system and is the only known planet to support life. It is a terrestrial planet, consisting of iron, oxygen, silicon, magnesium, and nickel, with a solid and active surface. The Earth's atmosphere is composed of 78% nitrogen, 21% oxygen, and 1% other gases, which provides the perfect conditions for life to thrive. The Earth's distance from the Sun allows it to maintain liquid water, which is essential for life.

vocabulary.definition: The collection of eight planets and their moons in orbit around the sun, together with smaller bodies in the form of asteroids, meteoroids, and comets
A celestial body moving in an elliptical orbit around a star
The third planet from the sun and the only known planet to support life
The curved path of a celestial object or spacecraft around a star, planet, or moon
Positioned in or relating to the sky, or outer space as observed in astronomy

questions: Ask questions to check understanding of: The position of the Earth if it was further away from the sun
The position of the Earth in the Solar system and its significance for human habitation
Ask learners to write one thing they learned concerning the Earth in the Solar system
What are the three planets that are visible without a telescope
Constellations and their examples
Stars that appear to move across the skies in the night

sub_topics.title: The Size and the Shape of the Earth
competences: Use model of the earth to show the Earth's shape and size

teacher_steps: Learners will work in clusters and use the globe, maps and models to illustrate the Earth's shape
Learners to use online maps like Google maps to explore the Earth's shape distances
Observe images or Google Earth for real-world visual evidence of the Earth's
Use a ball and flashlight in a dark room show the Earth's shape. Place the ball in front of the flashlight and rotate it slowly
Let learners observe curved shadow, the lit side and the dark side
Let learners walk the scaled model of the Earth's circumference such as (1 meter represents 1000 km)
learner_steps: Describing the shape and the size of the Earth
Using globe, maps and models to illustrate Earth's shape
Observing and explaining what they see
Walking measured distance to understand Earth's circumference
instructional_blocks.short_notes: The Earth is not a perfect sphere but an oblate spheroid (spherical), meaning it is slightly flattened at the poles and bulging at the equator. The term 'geoid' refers to the Earth's shape as defined by mean sea level and the gravitational field. The Earth's circumference is about 40,075 kilometers around the equator. The average diameter of the Earth is about 12,742 kilometers. The equatorial diameter is about 12,756 kilometers, while the polar diameter is about 12,714 kilometers. The total surface area of the Earth is about 510 million square kilometers and its volume is approximately 1 trillion cubic kilometers. The Earth's radius is approximately 6,371 kilometers.

teacher_steps: Watch a video or images of the lunar eclipse
Identify and sketch the round shadow cast by Earth on the moon
Use Google Earth or satellite images to observe Earth from space
Research how airplanes and ships travel in curved paths
In an open area learners observe a distant object (airplane or tall building)
Place a stick upright in the ground at noon and measure its shadow
Compare results with learners from another distant location and analyse the difference in shadows
learner_steps: Analysing evidence of the Earth's shape
Recording how distant objects disappear from bottom up
instructional_blocks.short_notes: The Earth's shape is an oblate spheroid, meaning it is approximately spherical in shape but slightly flattened at the poles and bulging at the equator. Evidence of the Earth's Sphericity includes: Ship's visibility over the horizon, the moon's eclipse/lunar eclipse, Aerial Photography, Circumnavigation of the Earth. Analysis of the evidence of the shape of the Earth is important for various reasons, among them include: understanding our planet, confirming scientific theories, for navigation and transportation, for GIS purposes, for climate modelling and weather forecasting. The Earth's spherical shape influences climate and weather patterns, which affect agriculture, water resource and daily life.

teacher_steps: Use a ball and flashlight to simulate Earths rotation and how it causes day and night
Discuss why the sun rises earlier in some places
Use a world map to analyse climate differences
Discuss how the Earths shape affects seasons, sunlight and climate
Use maps or google Earth to trace airplane routes discuss why flights follow curved paths
Research how time zones affect foreign business and communication
learner_steps: Relating the Earth's spherical shape and size to natural and human activities
instructional_blocks.short_notes: The Earth is approximately spherical in shape, meaning it is roughly circular in shape when viewed from space. The Earth's spherical shape is characterised by its curved surface, with all points on the surface being approximately equal distance from the equator. The Earth's spherical shape plays a crucial role in many natural phenomena such as climate patterns, ocean currents and the behaviour of the atmosphere. The Earth's size affects many aspects of our lives including: climate, geography and the availability of natural resources. Natural activities refer to the processes and events that occur naturally on the Earth without human intervention. Human activities refer to the actions and processes carried out by humans that impacts the Earth's environment, ecosystems, and natural resources.

sub_topics.short_notes: The Earth our home planet is a vast and complex system that has fascinated humans for centuries. Understanding the size and shape of our planet is essential for grasping various geographical concepts, from navigating and mapping to climate and global connections. The Earth is not a perfect sphere, instead, it has an oblate spheroid shape, meaning it is slightly flattened at the poles and bulging at the equator. This shape is due to the planet's rotation.

vocabulary.definition: A shape that is approximately spherical but slightly flattened at the poles and bulging at the equator
The Earth's shape as defined by mean sea level and the gravitational field
The distance around the Earth
The distance across the Earth through its center

questions: Quizzes to determine learners understanding of the key concepts such as the Earth's shape, size and circumference
Observe learners' participation in groups as they discuss the Earth's shape and size
Ask learners to create concept maps or diagrams to illustrate their understanding of the Earth's shape and size
Learners to use clay, play dough, or papier Mache or any materials found locally to create the Earth object
Observe learners' participation in activities
Check if learners can explain their findings clearly
Check if learners are able to collect and analyse data

sub_topics.title: Movements of the Earth
competences: Apply knowledge of earth movements in different situations

teacher_steps: Display maps and diagrams showing the movement of the earth
Learners to study the video showing movement of the earth
Learners work in pairs to mimic rotation and revolution of the Earth
One learner stands still (representing the sun) and the other one (representing the Earth) spins to show rotation and moves round to show revolution
Prepare learners for a rotation relay
Select 5-6 learners to represent major locations (Zambia, Kenya, USA, India and Japan)
Each learner stands at a different point in a large circle around the sun
Create four groups each representing a season (summer, Autumn, winter, spring)
learner_steps: Examining the movements of the Earth and their effects
Discussing what happens when the earth rotates and revolves
Explaining what happens in their season
instructional_blocks.short_notes: The Earth moves in space in two distinct ways namely Rotation and Revolution. Rotation is the spinning of the Earth on its tilted axis. The Earth rotates from West to East once in every 24 hours. The effects of rotation of the Earth results in day and night, difference in time at different lines of longitude, deflection of winds and ocean currents, revolution of the moon around the earth and tidal changes. Revolution refers to Earth's orbital motion around the Sun every 365¼ days. The effects of revolution of the Earth includes: changes in the length of day and night from day to day, changes in the altitude of the midday sun and changes in seasons. The combined effect of Earth's revolution, rotation, and axial tilt produce seasonal variations in the North and South Hemispheres.

teacher_steps: Learners in groups choose an event (planting season, religious festival, international business meeting, sports activity)
Using charts and weather charts or calendars learners identify best time of the year
Consider time zones if it is an international event
Discuss how Earth's movements affect the event's success
learner_steps: Using knowledge of Earth movements to plan for specific events
Planning events considering seasons and time zones
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Earth's rotations affect time and daily activities. It causes day and night, influencing work schedules, travel and business hours. It creates time zones affecting global communication and event planning. Earths revolution determines seasons and affects farming, influences tourism and outdoor events.

sub_topics.short_notes: The Earth's primary movements are Rotation (spinning on its axis) from West to East once in every 24 hours causing day and night and Revolution (orbiting around the Sun) once in 365¼ days causing seasons. These movements significantly impact life on Earth by causing day and night cycles, seasons, and variations in the amount of sunlight different regions receive, influencing climate and weather patterns.

vocabulary.definition: The spinning of the Earth on its tilted axis
Earth's orbital motion around the Sun
An imaginary line through the center of Earth around which it rotates
The path Earth takes around the Sun

questions: Ask questions to assess understanding of: Rotation and Revolution of the earth
Effects of the Rotation and Revolution of the earth
Learners to make a model of the Earth depicting its movement
Observe in discussions if the learners can explain their observations
Monitor learners' engagement and participation in the activities

sub_topics.title: Earth's Graticule
competences: Use latitudes and longitudes to locate places on the globe
Apply calculations of time and longitude in different situations
Apply calculations of the midday sun in real-life

teacher_steps: Display globe or world map or short video to arouse curiosity
Learners in groups use rubber bands or strings and markers to conduct a globe or ball activity
One learner holds a globe or ball while another wraps a string or rubber band around it horizontally (latitudes)
Another wraps a string or rubber band around it vertically (longitude)
Learners in groups to make model of the globe showing latitudes and longitudes using locally available materials
learner_steps: Using strings and markers on globe/ball
instructional_blocks.short_notes: The Earth's graticule is the network of lines and coordinates that crisscross the globe, providing a framework for mapping, navigation and geographic reference. Latitude is the angular distance of a point on the earth's surface, measured in degrees from the centre of the earth. Lines of latitudes are imaginary lines that run east-west around the Earth, parallel to the equator. Important latitudes: the Arctic Circle (66½°), the tropic of Cancer (23½° N), the Equator (0°), the Tropic of Capricorn (23½°) and the Antarctic Circle (66½°). Longitude is the angular distance measured in degrees along the equator east or west of the Greenwich Meridian (0°). They are lines that run from North Pole to the South Pole. Longitudes are used in determining local time in relation to the Greenwich Meridian Time (GMT).

teacher_steps: Display maps/globe showing International Date Line
Learners in groups to identify the international date line and countries or towns near it
Learners to be placed in small groups to act out travelers flying between Fiji to Hawaii (gaining a day), New Zealand to the USA (losing a day)
They use flight tickets (made in class) showing departure and arrive dates
learner_steps: Discussing what happens when crossing the date line
instructional_blocks.short_notes: The International Date Line is an imaginary line on the Earth's surface that runs North to South through the middle of the Pacific Ocean following 180 degrees Meridian. It separates two consecutive calendar days with locations to the West of the line being one day ahead of the locations to the East. When crossing the date line from East to West one loses a day and by crossing from West to East a day is gained. When travelling eastwards from Greenwich, time is gained by 24 hours until one reaches the Meridian 180° E. Similarly, when going westwards from Greenwich, time is lost by 24 hours when one reaches 180°W.

teacher_steps: Cluster the learners in groups
Provide the globe or grid and digital maps for learners to identify latitudes and longitudes
Learners to use latitudes and longitudes in locating and pin pointing places on the maps
learner_steps: Locating places on the map using latitudes and longitudes
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Places on the Earth's surface can be located using imaginary lines (Latitudes and longitudes). Latitudes runs from west to east, parallel to the equator. Longitudes runs north and south passing through the poles. The intersection of the two set of lines (latitudes and longitudes) pin-point and indicate precise location of a place on the Globe.

teacher_steps: Setting up a computer laboratory (GIS)
Pairing of learners to conduct the learning in the computer laboratory
Learners to search and locate places on the globe using GIS
learner_steps: Locating places on the globe using Geographic Information System (GIS)
instructional_blocks.short_notes: GIS is a computer-based system that integrate geographic data with spatial analysis and visualization. It is used in locating places by pinpointing specific locations using geographic coordinates such as latitudes and longitude.

teacher_steps: Learners clustered to collaborate on the use of gained knowledge
Display charts, maps, pictures showing spatial distribution and time zones
Learners to identify areas or situations where to use gained knowledge and skills
Use GPS to ascertain coordinates of important local areas such as sporting fields, clinic
Learners to create plans using GIS knowledge
learner_steps: Using knowledge of location of places in various ways (Route planning, reading maps and using GPS, time zone differences)
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Latitudes and longitudes are used to locate places, calculation of time and distance. GPS can be used in planning for route use, search for location when in a new place. Time zoning such as planning for different activities such as conferences, calls, meetings, travel. Generate and read maps. GPS could be used in tracking of cars, animals in a national parks, theft cases.

teacher_steps: Learners to be put in small groups to discuss use of longitudes to calculate local and standard time
Display maps/charts or globe showing local and foreign cities and their longitudes
Learners to make comparisons of time and standard time for each town
Using longitude of each town, calculate the local time for some identified towns
learner_steps: Calculating local and standard time using longitudes
Calculating local times for different towns
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Standard time is where each country adopts the local time of its Central Meridian e.g Zambia adopted the 30°E longitude as its Central Meridian. Time Zones are divisions into which the World is divided, that is, 24 belts, each 15° of longitude wide. Longitudes are used to calculate local time of places in relation to the Greenwich Mean Time (GMT). If the GMT is known, local time can easily be found by either adding or subtracting the difference in the number of hours from the given longitudes. Therefore, to the East; Gain = Add (E.G.A) and to the West; Lose = Subtract (W.L.S). The Earth makes one complete rotation of 360° in one day or 24 hours; hence it passes through 15° in every 1 hour. Since the Earth passes through 15° in every 1 hour, this means it passes through 1° every 4 minutes.

teacher_steps: Learners to be placed in small groups and use a World Time Zone Map to locate their country
Compare time differences with other locations
Use the stick and sun method (shadow experiment)
Place a stick in the ground observe local noon when the shadow is shortest
Record exact time when the shadow is shortest
Calculate the time difference between their local noon and GMT
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Calculating longitudes involve determining the difference between local time and Greenwich Mean Time (GMT). Determine the local time and GMT of a respective location. Find the difference in time between local time and GMT. Convert time difference to longitude by using the formula: Longitude = Time Difference × 15°

teacher_steps: Learners to be put in small groups to collaborate on use of time zones in different situations
Display Globe, Maps and Charts showing different town zones
Learners simulate planning an international flight considering different time zones
Learners role play as international business people scheduling meetings across time zones
Learners track international news or sports schedules
learner_steps: Using time zones in different situations
Calculating time differences for meetings and travel
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Time zones are regions on Earth that follow a uniform standard time at a specific meridian. Time zones facilitate efficient communication, transportation and economic transactions across the Globe. Time zones are integrated in the development of technological advancements especially modern technologies such as GPS systems and computer networks which rely on precise time keeping and coordination. They are vital in Scientific Research, Navigation and mapping.

teacher_steps: Assign groups with cities with different known latitudes
Distribute maps with lines of latitudes
Provide learners with latitude calculation worksheets for different cities
Use GPS devices or smart phones to measure latitude and calculate distances
instructional_blocks.short_notes: The Earth is a sphere with 360° of latitude, 180° N/S from the Equator. One degree of latitude = 111km. The formula for calculating: Distance = (Difference in Latitude) × 111km. Latitudes are used to calculate distances places on different hemispheres and same hemispheres. Pilots, Sailors and hikers use latitudes to estimate distance and navigate.

teacher_steps: Learners prepare guides, explaining how to use latitudes to calculate distances
Practical exercises to help learners reinforce their understanding
Using Google Maps and GPS to teach family members
Drawing maps to show routes visually
Help family members plan their journeys
learner_steps: Guiding family and community members to use calculations to arrive at distances, routes, directions
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Latitude is used to measure the distance North or South of the equator ranging from 0° to 90°. Learners are encouraged to create a map of local area labelling important landmarks and their latitudes.

teacher_steps: Learners to be put in pairs
Display charts showing position of the sun in different places
Provide the Globe to indicate Earth's inclination
Display Maps indicating various location of towns
Learners to look up at any building or tall tree
Learners to estimate Angle of elevation
learner_steps: Calculating the position of the midday sun (Angle of Elevation)
Estimating angle of elevation between ground and line of sight
instructional_blocks.short_notes: The angle of elevation is the angle between the horizontal plane and a line of sight to an object above the horizon. Angle of elevation is based on the position of the midday sun and position of the place. When the overhead sun and the place are in the same hemisphere, subtract the overhead sun angle from the place and the difference subtract from 90°. When the sun is overhead at the equator use the formula 90° - N where N is the latitude angle. When the overhead sun and place are in different Hemisphere add the overhead sun angle and the angle of the place then subtract from 90°.

teacher_steps: Learners to be put in small groups
Display a map showing the relationship between latitude, longitude and the midday sun's position
Learners to calculate the latitude of a place using the position of the midday sun
learner_steps: Calculating the latitude of a place using the position of the midday sun
Using sun's elevation angle and declination
Using formula: 90° – (Sun Elevation Angle + Sun's Declination) for Southern hemisphere OR 90° – (Sun Elevation Angle - Sun's Declination) for Northern hemisphere
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Latitudes are calculated by using sun's elevation angle and the sun's declination. Measure the elevation angle between the sun and the vertical at noon when the sun is highest in the sky. Determine the sun's Declination which is angular distance from the equator. Determine the hemisphere whether it is Northern or Southern.

teacher_steps: Learners put in small groups to discuss the use of the midday sun
Display Graphs, Globe, Maps and Charts showing different real-world applications
Learners carry a case study of a real-life scenario such as solar Panel installation
learner_steps: Using calculations of midday sun in various ways (Estimate time, determine directions, solar panel placement, designing energy efficient buildings)
instructional_blocks.short_notes: The angle of elevation helps determine the height of building, mountains or other structures. The angle of elevation is used in navigation systems such as GPS, to calculate positions. In engineering, angle of elevation is used to design and construct buildings, bridges and other infrastructure.

sub_topics.short_notes: The Globe comprises of a number of imaginary lines that crisscross it. These lines constitute of what is called the Earth's graticule. It is composed of two main sets of lines namely latitudes and longitudes. These lines are used to precisely measure and locate positions on the Earth's surface.

vocabulary.definition: The angular distance of a point on the earth's surface, measured in degrees from the centre of the earth
The angular distance measured in degrees along the equator east or west of the Greenwich Meridian
The prime meridian (0° longitude) passing through Greenwich, England
An imaginary line following 180° meridian where the date changes by one day
Greenwich Mean Time - the mean solar time at the Royal Observatory in Greenwich, London
Geographic Information System - a computer-based system for spatial analysis and visualization

questions: Observe learners' participation in small groups as they identify and describe latitudes and longitudes
Quick question - I learnt that Latitudes and Longitudes are used for…
Observe learners as they demonstrate the crossing of the International Date Line
Verify if Learners understand what happens when the International Date Line is crossed
Observe learners create a coordinate grid on a piece of paper and plot points using coordinates
Observe learners using GIS to create a coordinate grid to locate places
Observe learners demonstrate the use of GPS in identification of new locations
Ask learners to calculate different local times using identified longitudes
Calculate departure and arrival times, taking into account time differences
Ascertain the best time to undertake various activities considering time zones
Show the relationship between latitude and distance
Calculate the distance between two points using latitude
Give brief reports on how they were able to guide family members
Observe the learner's participation in pairs as they estimate the angle of elevation
A city located at latitude of 40°N. Calculate the position of the midday sun on September 22nd, March 21st
On June 21st the sun is directly overhead at the Tropic of cancer (23.5°N). Calculate the solar noon angle for a location at 30°N latitude

sub_topics.title: The Earth's Atmosphere

teacher_steps: Take the learners outside and let them observe the sky
Discuss in pairs what makes up the air and how the atmosphere helps us
Learners in small groups, each group representing one layer of the atmosphere
They will use textbooks or internet to gather information
Each group to present their findings
learner_steps: Describing the Earth's Atmosphere (stating the major layers)
Observing the sky and describing experiences
instructional_blocks.short_notes: The first layer of the Earth's atmosphere is the Troposphere that extends from the surface to between 7km at the poles and 17 km at the equator. The Troposphere is the lowest layer with decreasing temperature with height. It is the layer we live in and where weather happens. The second layer is the Stratosphere found between 12km to 50km above. The temperature in this layer increases with height. The stratosphere houses the ozone layer which absorbs the ultraviolet (UV) rays from the sun. The Mesosphere is the third layer and is above the stratosphere lying between 50km and 80km. The Thermosphere is the next layer after the Mesosphere which lies 80km and above. The Ionosphere is an electrically charged layer which is used for communication and navigation purposes. The Exosphere is the layer without gases and is mainly used in modern artificial satellite launch.

teacher_steps: Learners in small groups use the prior information to prepare the demonstration on the importance of the atmosphere
Each group to demonstrate to the whole class how they will share information with family and community
Learners will talk to family members about what they learned in class
Learners can organise a short talk with neighbors about air pollution
They can create short skits or stories and perform them
learner_steps: Sharing with family and community members on the role of the atmosphere
instructional_blocks.short_notes: The atmosphere provides essential elements that support life on Earth such as gases, water vapour. The atmosphere regulates temperature and protects people from harmful radiation. Family discussions about climate change are a critical factor influencing communities' perceptions and behaviors. Encourage learners to discuss climate change impacts with family members, sharing their knowledge, experiences and concerns.

teacher_steps: The teacher in collaboration with the learners form the conservation club
Learners identify the area in the school surrounding where trees can be planted
Learners write posters on tree planting, reducing energy use, recycling
Learners can organise a day to plant trees at school and home
Suggest that friends or families share rides
Organise and participate in clean up campaigns
learner_steps: Engaging in measures that protect the Earth's atmosphere (planting trees, using sustainable transportation, reducing energy use, recycling)
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Solar Energy: Encourage the use of solar lamps and chargers. Tree Planting: Join or start a tree-planting initiative. Sustainable Transportation: Encourage walking or biking to school. Reduce Plastic Use: Use alternatives to plastic. Gardening Projects: Start or join community gardens. Upcycling Workshops: Teach others how to repurpose old items. Renewable Energy Projects: Build solar cookers or wind turbines.

sub_topics.short_notes: The Earth's atmosphere is a mixture of gases that surrounds the earth. It helps make life possible by providing air, shielding from harmful ultraviolet (UV) radiation coming from the sun, trapping heat to warm the planet and preventing extreme temperature differences between day and night. The Atmosphere is a mixture of nitrogen (78%), Oxygen (21%) and other gases (1%) that surrounds the Earth.

vocabulary.definition: The layer of gases surrounding the Earth
The lowest layer of atmosphere where weather occurs
The layer containing the ozone layer that absorbs UV radiation
A region of the stratosphere that absorbs harmful ultraviolet radiation
Ultraviolet radiation from the sun that can be harmful to living organisms

questions: Monitor learner's participation as they discuss the layers of the Atmosphere
Research and write a report on the role of the atmosphere
Analyse the importance of the Troposphere
Observe learners as they give reports on how they shared information with family and community
Observe learners' participation in class discussions
Observe learners' participation in initiatives that help protect the atmosphere

sub_topics.title: Weather and Climate
competences: Apply knowledge of weather and climate in daily activities

instructional_blocks.hook: Your community is facing frequent floods. What weather related data would you collect to predict future floods, and how would you help the community prepare?
teacher_steps: Prepare a resource corner with weather instruments, charts and newspapers
Cluster learners for interactive activities such as role plays
Learners roleplay where they pretend to be weather forecasters or climate scientists
Learners create a T-Chart where they list daily weather observations vs long term climate pattern
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Weather refers to the state of the atmosphere at a particular time and place. It is a short term process and can change at any time and affects daily activities. Climate is the average weather conditions of a place over a long period of time. It is long term, consistent over time, affects ecosystems. When we say it is hot, cloudy or windy we are referring to weather. When we say hot summers or cold winters we are referring to climate. These patterns of weather are recorded for many years to come up with climate of the region.

teacher_steps: Learners in clusters to conduct inquiry-based research on elements of weather
Report to the whole class on the results
Conduct a tour to a weather station to observe weather instruments
Carry out outdoor weather walks to allow for real-life observation
Give learners observation sheets
Learners go outside to observe, record and describe current weather conditions
Make models of some weather instruments
learner_steps: Identifying the various elements of weather and climate (temperature, humidity, rainfall, cloud cover)
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Temperature: Measure how hot or cold the atmosphere is measured by Thermometer. Rainfall: Water falling from the atmosphere measured by Rain gauge. Wind speed and direction: Air in motion measured by Cup Anemometer and Wind vane. Humidity: The amount of water vapour in the air measured by Hygrometer. Cloud cover: The amount and type of cloud present measured by observation. Sunshine: The intensity and duration of sunlight measured by Sunshine Recorder. Atmospheric air pressure: The weight of air exerted on earth's surface measured by Barometer.

teacher_steps: Create different activity stations such as research table with books
Use school spaces to observe local weather conditions
Learners to use weather tools such as thermometers, wind vanes and rain gauges
Focus on environmental features such as vegetation, clouds and water bodies
Learners to rotate through focus points
Note nearby waterbodies, vegetation, buildings, landforms
Learners can now gather to discuss what they noticed
learner_steps: Identifying factors influencing weather and climate (latitude, altitude, seasons, continentality, prevailing winds)
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Latitudes: areas near the equator are warmer than those farther away. Altitude: temperatures reduces as one ascends. Water bodies such as lakes and oceans make places cooler or warmer. Vegetation: forest areas cooler than open areas. Areas near the sea are cooler than those in the interior. Winds and ocean currents move heat and moist around the globe. Our environment influences the weather and human activities can also change local climates.

teacher_steps: Learners to be in small groups
Display map of Zambia showing different climate zones
Learners to discuss the climate of Zambia
Learners analyse and interpret the map based on temperature and precipitation, vegetation
Learners compare and contrast climate patterns
Learners to conduct field measurements at a local weather station
Conduct a survey of local vegetation
learner_steps: Analysing the climate of Zambia (Savanna, winds/Air masses, seasons)
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Zambia has Savanna type of vegetation characterized by tall grasses and scattered trees. Zambia has a tropical climate with three distinct seasons: cold season, hot season and rainy season. The South western part is drier and hotter, the Eastern region is cooler and wetter and the Northern region is warmer and more humid. Zambia experiences trade winds from the southeast which bring warm, moist air. During winter, easterly winds bring cool, dry air. Zambia is influenced by the tropical air mass with high temperatures and high humidity.

teacher_steps: Put learners in small groups to prepare recording sheets
Learners to prepare and plan for suitable locations for outdoor activities
Field trip to weather stations to enable learners observe and record weather patterns
Measure wind direction using locally made materials
learner_steps: Using knowledge on weather and climate (planning outdoor activities, clothing and preparedness, health and wellbeing, travel, agriculture)
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Check weather forecasts before engaging in outdoor activities. Encourage learners to dress appropriately for weather. Learners to stay hydrated during outdoor activities. Plan for outdoor games on days with favourable weather. Learners to plant and maintain garden/field by incorporating knowledge of weather and climate. School tours should take into account weather and climate.

sub_topics.short_notes: Weather changes from time to time. Climate is more stable and involves average atmospheric conditions of a large area over a long period of time. In order to understand weather and climate, one needs to observe and interpret daily atmospheric conditions such as temperature, rainfall, wind and humidity.

vocabulary.definition: The state of the atmosphere at a particular time and place
The average weather conditions of a place over a long period of time
A measure of how hot or cold the atmosphere is
The amount of water vapour in the air
Water falling from the atmosphere in various forms
Vegetation type characterized by tall grasses and scattered trees

questions: Use a quick checklist while observing as learners roleplay
Conduct a quiz on weather and climate
Making of weather instrument models and create a mini weather station
Measure and record weather elements such as temperature, wind and rainfall
Use the maps, charts of Zambia to identify climatic regions based on temperature, precipitation and vegetation
Observe learners' participation and engagement during outdoor activities
Check learners use of knowledge of weather and climate in daily activities

sub_topics.title: Climate Change

teacher_steps: Put learners in groups
Display chart or pictures or show a short video clip of activities such as charcoal burning, agricultural activities and volcanic eruption
Learners study and discuss displayed charts, pictures or videos
Conduct an observation walk and let learners observe and identify natural features and human activities
Regroup and let groups share observations
Learners to record and report on the causes of climate change
learner_steps: Identifying the causes of climate change (Human causes: burning of fossil fuels, agriculture, deforestation; Natural causes: volcanic eruption)
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Climate change is as a result of natural and human forces. Human causes include: Burning of fossil fuels, Deforestation, Agriculture activities, Pollution. Natural causes include: El Niño Southern Oscillation (ENSO) effects, Solar irradiance, Volcanic eruptions, Plate tectonics and continental drift.

teacher_steps: Learners should sit in circles, groups or u-shaped for face-to-face interaction
Show short video/images of climate related risks and disasters
Learners to be in breakout spaces for each group to work
Provide learners with simple case studies showing climate change impacts in different regions
Each group to should make presentations on their case study findings
learner_steps: Analysing the impact of climate change on human activity (extreme temperatures, drought, heat waves, loss of habitat)
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Environmental impacts: Rising temperatures, Extreme weather events, Loss of biodiversity. Human impacts: Respiratory problems due to changes in air quality. Economic impacts: Damage to infrastructure, Agricultural decline, Loss of livestock, Energy crisis, Loss of livelihoods. Social impacts: Displacement of people, Vector borne diseases, Mental health impacts, Heat related illness, Conflicts of resources, Food security.

teacher_steps: Learners to share with family members real life experiences on the causes of climate change
learner_steps: Sharing information with family and community members on the causes and impact of climate change
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Family discussions about climate change are a critical factor influencing communities' perceptions and behaviors. Encourage learners to discuss climate change impacts with family members, sharing their knowledge, experiences and concerns. Engage learners and family members in joint projects. Invite learners to share personal stories or family experiences related to climate change. Learners to participate in community events involving climate change affairs.

sub_topics.short_notes: Climate change refers to long-term shifts in temperatures and weather patterns. Such shifts can be natural, due to changes in the sun's activity or large volcanic eruptions or can be due to human activities such as the burning of fossil fuels like coal, oil and gas.

vocabulary.definition: Long-term shifts in temperatures and weather patterns
Gases that trap heat in the atmosphere
Coal, oil, and gas formed from ancient organic matter
The clearing of forests for other land uses

questions: Observe learners' participation in discussions, ability to identify and explain causes
Ask learners to report on the causes and effects of climate change
Observe learner's participation as they brainstorm on the impacts
Check on the leaners' reports as they identify the impacts on environment, human life, economy
What did you learn about climate change?
How does climate change affects your family and community?
What actions can you take to reduce the impact of climate change?

sub_topics.title: Mitigation and Adaptation to Climate Change
competences: Practise mitigation measures to climate change
Adapt lifestyles to local climate risks and disasters

teacher_steps: Arrange learners in small groups for them to collaborate
Learners to discuss the mitigation measures to climate change
Each small group to share key ideas to the class
Key responses should be written down and similar ideas consolidated
learner_steps: Identifying mitigation measures to climate change (reducing waste, using renewable energy, conserving water, planting trees)
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Zambia is taking steps to reduce greenhouse gas emissions: Solar Energy - use of solar lamps and chargers. Tree Planting - join tree-planting initiatives. Climate Clubs - participate in climate clubs. Sustainable Transportation - walking or biking. Local Foods - support local farmers. Reduce Plastic Use - use alternatives. Education and Advocacy - spread awareness. Gardening Projects - community gardens. Upcycling Workshops - repurpose old items. Renewable Energy Projects - solar cookers, wind turbines.

teacher_steps: Divide learners into smaller groups
Discuss on how to participate in mitigating measures at individual and community levels
Learners can work in groups on sample projects: Reduce waste and promote recycling, School and community tree planting, Energy audit and conservation plan, School garden with climate smart farming
learner_steps: Engaging in Mitigation measures to Climate change
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Engaging in mitigation measures is essential at individual and community levels. Individual Level: Energy Efficiency & Renewable Energy, Sustainable Transportation, Waste Reduction & Sustainable Consumption, Food & Agriculture Choices. Community level: Participate in tree planting, Advocate for bike lanes and green spaces, Support local sustainability projects.

teacher_steps: Watch documentaries or educational films about climate change
Design a climate change bingo game
Organize a structured discussion or debate
Invite an environmental expert
Take a walk in a local park, forest, or nature reserve
Host a tree planting or community garden event
Take a tour of a local farm or sustainable energy facility
learner_steps: Sharing information on climate change with family and community members
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Sharing information on climate change means educating those around you about causes, effects, and solutions. It involves explaining the science behind global warming, its impact on environment, economy, and society, and the importance of collective action. This can include discussing carbon emissions, renewable energy, sustainable practices, and how individuals can reduce their carbon footprints. The goal is to raise awareness, inspire action, and encourage positive environmental behaviors.

teacher_steps: Divide learners in small groups
Each group represents an area, town or city facing climate change challenges
Each group randomly selects or is assigned a climate scenario
Groups design a community that incorporates adaptation strategies
Learners explore their local environment to identify real-world examples
Learners explore and look for adaptation features such as trees, permeable pavement, water-saving systems
learner_steps: Identifying ways of adapting to climate risks and disasters (growing drought resistant crops, water harvesting, having emergency plans)
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Build climate-resilient buildings on higher ground with improved drainage. Develop early warning systems. Keep emergency supplies ready. Water Conservation during dry season. Adjust to food shortages by growing vegetables, preserving food. Promote drought-resistant crops. Cope with extreme heat by planting trees. Protect against climate related diseases. Educate and involve local communities in adaptation strategies.

teacher_steps: Divide participants into small groups representing areas facing climate challenges
Each group designs a community incorporating adaptation strategies
Groups create visual representation on poster or whiteboard
Learners explore local environments to identify real-world examples
Document adaptation features
learner_steps: Engaging in adaptation measures to climate change
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Zambia is implementing strategies to adapt to changing climate: Build climate-resilient buildings. Develop early warning systems. Emergency preparedness. Water Conservation. Adjust to food shortages. Promote drought-resistant crops. Cope with extreme heat. Protect against climate related diseases. Educate and involve local communities.

sub_topics.short_notes: The topic focuses on efforts aimed at coming up with actions and strategies that can reduce or prevent long-term climate change risks. The goal of mitigation is to address the root causes of climate change. To build resilience, individuals and communities must adapt their lifestyles to local climate risks, ensuring they can withstand and recover from environmental hazards more effectively. Adaptation strategies include designing climate-resilient homes, adopting sustainable agricultural practices, modifying daily routines, and leveraging technology for early warnings.

vocabulary.definition: Actions to reduce or prevent greenhouse gas emissions
Adjustments to reduce vulnerability to climate change impacts
The ability to withstand and recover from climate-related hazards
Methods that meet current needs without compromising future generations

questions: Ask learners to propose measures to climate change
Ask learners to practice conservation measures within the school premises
Give two examples of how individuals can engage in climate change mitigation
How can businessmen contribute to climate change mitigation?
Should governments enforce strict laws to reduce carbon emissions?
I can personally contribute to climate change mitigation by…
Learners to visit community members and interview them then write a report
Let learners demonstrate activities on how individuals, families, and communities engage in combating climate change
Develop a sustainability action plan for family or community
Create a visual representation illustrating the impact of climate change
Observe learners as they prepare, plan for climate disaster observation
Observe learners as they make report presentations on findings
Assess ability to categorize and differentiate between mitigation and adaptation strategies
Observe learner participation in local adaptation efforts
